content:
3 min read
1 hour ago

--

A few years into this web dev journey, and I keep running into the same take: "AI writes the code now, so what’s even the point of learning to build?"
I get why people think that. On the surface, it looks true. You type a prompt, you get a component. Why would anyone pay a developer?
But here’s the question nobody’s asking: can everyone actually write that prompt?
Can everyone sit with a bug for three hours, chase it through five files, and figure out why it’s happening? Can everyone look at a business and know which button goes where, which feature actually drives conversions, and how to make a site look good and sell?
Not everyone. And that’s exactly where web dev still lives.
We’ve been here before
Think about banking before computers. To get hired as a banker, you needed to be exceptional at math — genuinely gifted, because every calculation ran through your head.
Then calculators showed up. Suddenly banks weren’t hiring the sharpest mental mathematicians anymore. They were hiring people who understood how money worked and knew how to use the tool in front of them. Basic math skills, paired with real understanding, became the bar — not raw computational power.
AI is doing the same thing to web development right now. It’s not erasing the need for developers. It’s changing what "being good" actually means.
Syntax was never the point
I used to think web dev was about memorizing syntax — the kind of thing you see in movies, someone typing furiously with a wall of code scrolling by.
It isn’t. Not anymore, and honestly, maybe never really was.
Web dev today is about increasing sales. It’s about putting content out that communicates clearly enough that people relate to it and stick around. It’s about logic, flow, and understanding the business behind the screen — not just the code on it.
If you still think this field is purely about syntax, I’d genuinely encourage you to look closer. Study the logic. Study the flow. Study how a business actually works. That’s what gets you hired now — not how fast you can type a for-loop.
The part nobody posts about
Here’s the honest part.
There have been moments I wanted to quit. Real ones. Not the dramatic "one bad day" kind — the slow, grinding kind that builds up after months of pushing.
I haven’t quit, because I’ve put in too much time and energy to walk away now. And I’ve learned something in the process: whatever skill you pick up, it looks deceptively simple at the start. The market looks wide open. It looks easy.
Then you spend real time in it — weeks turning into months turning into years — and you start hitting things you never planned for. Doubt creeps in from inside your own head. It creeps in from outside too — from the internet, from your environment, from people who mean well but don’t get it.
But if you keep pushing, you get there. Not instantly. Not painlessly. But for sure.
If you’re a developer wrestling with where this industry is headed, I’d love to hear how you’re navigating it — drop your thoughts below.
